title: Winners and Losers of Globalization: Sixteen Challenges for Measurement and Theory
abstract: The goal of this chapter is to summarize the state of the art in research in international trade and global production, and discuss issues relevant to European policymakers. Much of recent research on globalization is primarily empirical, owing to the proliferation of available data. We begin by discussing recent advances in measuring the causes and effects of globalization, and discussing the particular data challenges that have emerged. We then turn to theories of trade and global production, first summarizing the conclusions on which there is a broad consensus in the field. We discuss new insights that may be relevant for policy-makers, and open research questions.
summary_en: This is a handbook chapter summarising the state of trade-and-globalisation research for European policymakers. It reviews recent advances in measuring the causes and effects of globalisation, the data challenges that have emerged, and the theoretical frameworks that have dominated trade economics. It identifies sixteen open questions — measurement and theory — that remain despite the explosion of firm-level trade data. A valuable overview for policymakers wanting to understand what economics now knows (and doesn't) about globalisation's winners and losers.
data_used: Survey chapter; no original empirical data.
policy_relevance: An accessible reference for Hungarian trade-policy staff (ITM, Pénzügyminisztérium, MFM) who want a rigorous overview of globalisation research. Useful framing for debates about EU trade agreements, industrial policy, and supply-chain resilience. Caveat: a survey from 2017; ensuing research (COVID-19 supply chains, great-power trade wars, reshoring) has rendered some parts dated.
